dossier.event_summary: Apple reported Q2 FY24 revenue of $90.8B (down 4% YoY) with EPS up 0.7% YoY, and the board authorized an additional $110B share repurchase program plus a 4% dividend increase.
dossier.reasoning_chain:
FOR taking action: The headline number on this 8-K is the $110B buyback authorization, which is the largest single buyback authorization on record for any US company at this time. Buybacks of this magnitude are a direct, mechanical bid under the stock over coming quarters and a strong signal of capital-return intent and balance-sheet confidence from management. Buyback announcements of this size on a mega-cap with deep liquidity historically tend to produce a positive next-session reaction, and the EPS beat-on-shrinking-revenue setup is consistent with management leaning further into buybacks to support EPS. Services at $23.87B continues to grow as a higher-margin mix, which is the structurally bullish part of the print. Filing hit at 16:30 ET — i.e. just after the close — so the market reaction is going to play out in after-hours and the next regular session, giving us time to wait for a confirmation candle rather than chasing AH liquidity at thin spreads.

AGAINST taking action: Revenue is down 4% YoY and the print is not a clean fundamental beat — it is a capital-return story bolted onto a soft top line. iPhone at $45.96B is the swing factor and the YoY decline there is the real concern; a buyback does not fix demand. The stock is mega-cap and heavily owned, so the marginal new buyer from this event is smaller than for a less-followed name. Most of all, on the right-side framework I should not pre-position into the gap based on a headline I cannot confirm has actually moved price yet; I have no after-hours quote, no next-day open, no volume, no follow-through. Acting on the 8-K body alone is left-side trading.

Sizing and confirmation: The right move is to NOT submit an intent now and instead wait for tomorrow's session to confirm. The confirmation I want is: (a) a positive gap that holds above the prior close ($220.50) through the first 30–60 minutes, (b) above-average volume relative to AAPL's 20-day average, and (c) no immediate fade back through the prior close. If those clear, the smallest defensible probe is 1 share at market within the $500 single-order cap (AAPL ~$220 -> well inside the cap), scaling to a second probe only if the stock holds the day-1 high into day 2. Invalidation: a fill of the prior close from above on day 1, or any close back below $220.50, kills the thesis — at that point the market has rejected the buyback as a catalyst and there is no edge left.

Mode: notify. I want a human (or the next loop iteration once we have post-event price data) to look at this, not a queued dry-run intent built on stale pre-event price.

alternatives_considered.rejected_because: No post-event price observed — only the pre-event $220.50 print. Submitting based on the 8-K body alone is left-side, pre-confirmation trading and violates the framework.

alternatives_considered.action: buy full size into the announcement
alternatives_considered.rejected_because: Violates probe-then-scale sizing, and a soft revenue print under the buyback headline is not the kind of evidence that justifies full sizing.

alternatives_considered.action: sell short on weak revenue / iPhone YoY decline
alternatives_considered.rejected_because: Asymmetric tail risk against a $110B buyback authorization on a mega-cap is severe; the mechanical bid alone makes shorting into this event a poor risk/reward and would require overwhelming countervailing evidence I do not have.
There is a real, sizable catalyst here; the correct response is to wait for confirmation, not to permanently pass.

alternatives_considered.action: queue a dry_run buy intent now to be evaluated at next open
alternatives_considered.rejected_because: Dry_run intent encodes a price/size decision built on pre-event data; better to emit a notify signal and let the next loop, with actual post-event quotes, decide sizing.

dossier.no_action_reason: Right-side discipline: the only price I have ($220.50) is pre-event, and the filing hit after the close. I want to see tomorrow's open, the gap, and the first 30–60 minutes of volume/price action before committing capital. The thesis is plausibly bullish (record $110B buyback + dividend hike) but the revenue print is soft, so confirmation matters more than usual. Emit as notify so a human or the next event loop can re-evaluate against actual post-event quotes.

review.reasoning:
The operator was presented with a high-salience 8-K (record $110B buyback authorization + dividend hike, set against a soft revenue print) and only a single pre-event price point ($220.50). The decision to NOT submit an order intent and instead emit a notify signal is the correct process call under the stated right-side, event-driven framework.

On information adequacy: the operator correctly identifies that the available info is structurally insufficient for a sizing decision. There is no after-hours quote, no next-session open, no volume context, no 20-day average to compare against. The framework requires post-event price confirmation, and the operator has none. Acting on the 8-K body alone would be left-side, pre-confirmation trading — the operator names this explicitly and refuses.

On reasoning chain: the FOR/AGAINST decomposition is genuinely balanced rather than performative. The bull case (mechanical buyback bid, services mix, EPS support) is acknowledged but not over-weighted; the bear case (revenue -4%, iPhone weakness, capital-return-bolted-onto-soft-fundamentals) is treated as material. The conclusion — wait for confirmation — follows directly from the framework rather than from a guess about direction.

On alternatives: five distinct alternatives are considered, including the symmetric short case and two intermediate actions (small probe, dry_run queue). Each rejection is justified on framework grounds, not on outcome prediction. The rejection of the dry_run queue is particularly well-reasoned: encoding sizing now against stale pre-event price would propagate a stale decision forward; notify defers the decision to a state where better information exists.

On confidence calibration: 0.60 is appropriate. The operator is confident about the *process* call (do not act now) but appropriately uncertain about the eventual direction, and 0.60 reflects exactly that — meaningful but not high. A higher number would have been miscalibrated given the soft revenue print.

On skills/tools: none invoked, which is correct — there is no sizing decision to support, so a sizing-oriented skill would be inappropriate. Emitting notify mode is consistent with the no_action_reason.

On the proposed intent: null intent with a notify mode is internally consistent with the stated rationale and with the framework's prohibition on pre-confirmation trading.

The only minor critique is that the pre-defined confirmation criteria (gap holds above $220.50, above-average volume, no immediate fade) and invalidation criteria (close back below $220.50) are stated as forward instructions for the next loop, but the operator does not formally hand them off as structured fields — they live in prose. This is a minor structural quibble, not a process error.
